L1.area: Consensus block proof verification and validator quorum checking

L2.description: An integer truncation vulnerability exists in the blockchain consensus layer. Signer slot indices from a bit set are cast from a larger integer type to a smaller one without range validation. An attacker can craft a proof containing out-of-range signer indices that, after truncation, collide with valid in-range slots. This inflates the apparent number of unique signers, allowing a malicious validator to bypass the Byzantine fault tolerance quorum threshold with far fewer actual signing keys.

L3.area: Block proof verification primitives for skip-block proofs, Tendermint round proofs, and equivocation (double-vote) proofs
L3.description: An integer truncation vulnerability exists in the blockchain consensus layer where signer slot indices from a multi-signature bit set are cast from a larger integer type to a 16-bit slot number without range validation. An attacker can craft block proofs containing out-of-range signer indices that, after truncation, collide with valid in-range slots. This inflates the apparent number of unique signers, allowing a malicious validator to bypass the two-thirds-plus-one quorum threshold with far fewer actual signing keys.

ground_truth.reason: BitSet signer indices are cast from usize to u16 without bounds checking, allowing out-of-range indices (spaced by 65536) to inflate the apparent signer count while colliding onto the same in-range slot, bypassing the quorum threshold.
